name: Bizkarreta (SP) - Aldudes (FR) via Sorogain
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Erro are situated in the Pyrenees of Navarra, Spain, characterized by mountainous terrain and lush valleys. The region features the Erro and Esteribar valleys, connected by the N-135 regional road, which provides well-maintained surfaces for cycling. Ancient woodlands of oaks, birches, and pines line many paths, offering scenic beauty. The area is known for challenging climbs, including the ascent to Alto de Erro, making it a destination for road cyclists seeking elevation gain. Best road cycling routes around Erro The most popular road cycling route is Roncesvalles-Col de Orgambide (FR) – western loop through the Sierra de Irati, a 49.9 miles (80.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 49 minutes to complete. This route offers a demanding ride through the western Sierra de Irati. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Bizkarreta (SP) - Aldudes (FR) via Sorogain, a difficult 29.1 miles (46.8 km) path. This route traverses varied terrain, including climbs via Sorogain. Local road cyclists also love the Alto de Erro Ascent – Rabia Bridge, Zubiri loop from Aurizberri, a 54.7 miles (88.1 km) trail leading through the Alto de Erro mountain pass, often completed in about 4 hours 35 minutes. Road cycling around Erro is defined by Pyrenean mountain passes, ancient woodlands, and the Erro and Esteribar valleys. The network offers options primarily for moderate to difficult ability levels. The routes in Erro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.96 stars from over 14 reviews. More than 1200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Erro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Erro?
A: Erro offers a diverse selection of over 20 road cycling routes. These routes primarily cater to moderate and difficult skill levels, with 8 moderate and 13 difficult options available for exploration.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Erro?
A: The Pyrenean region around Erro is generally best for road cycling from late spring through early autumn. During these months, the weather is typically more stable, and mountain passes are clear of snow, offering ideal conditions to enjoy the challenging climbs and scenic landscapes.

Q: Are there routes suitable for advanced road cyclists in Erro?
A: Yes, Erro is well-suited for advanced road cyclists, with 13 routes classified as difficult. These routes feature significant elevation gains and demanding climbs, such as the ascent to Alto de Erro. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984999254/roncesvalles-col-de-orgambide-fr-western-loop-through-the-sierra-de-irati" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roncesvalles-Col de Orgambide (FR) – western loop through the Sierra de Irati&lt;/a&gt;, a 80.4 km route with over 1900 meters of elevation gain.

Q: Are there any loop road bike routes around Erro?
A: Many of the road cycling routes in Erro are designed as loops, allowing cyclists to start and finish in the same location. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5348674" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Erro Ascent – Rabia Bridge, Zubiri loop from Aurizberri&lt;/a&gt; is an 88.1 km loop that takes you through the Alto de Erro mountain pass.

Q: What are the typical lengths and elevations for road cycling routes in Erro?
A: Road cycling routes in Erro vary significantly in length and elevation, reflecting the mountainous Pyrenean terrain. You can expect routes ranging from approximately 45 km to nearly 100 km, with elevation gains often exceeding 1000 meters. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40321052" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ochagavía – Laza Pass (1,129 m) loop from Aurizberri&lt;/a&gt; is 97.5 km long with almost 1800 meters of ascent.

Q: What scenic viewpoints can I find along road cycling routes in Erro?
A: The region around Erro is renowned for its panoramic views, especially from mountain passes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4817151" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Erro Pass&lt;/a&gt; itself offers stunning vistas of the surrounding Pyrenees and the lush Erro valley. Other notable passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/717452" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibañeta Pass&lt;/a&gt; also provide excellent viewpoints.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest to explore while road cycling in Erro?
A: Yes, Erro is steeped in history, particularly due to its location on the Camino de Santiago (French Way). Cyclists can encounter historical sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/717452" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roland Monument&lt;/a&gt; near Ibañeta Pass, which commemorates the legendary French hero. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1014822" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roland Fountain&lt;/a&gt; is another historical point of interest.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes in Erro?
A: While specific cafes are not listed for every route, the N-135 regional road connects towns like Erro and Zubiri, where you can typically find amenities for refreshments. Given the region's popularity with cyclists and pilgrims on the Camino de Santiago, small villages along the routes often provide opportunities for a break.

Q: Is public transport available to access road cycling routes near Erro?
A: Erro is located in a rural, mountainous region. While local bus services might connect some towns, direct public transport specifically tailored for accessing remote cycling route starting points may be limited. It's advisable to check local transport schedules for the specific towns you plan to visit, such as those along the N-135 road.

Q: What are the parking options for road cyclists in Erro?
A: Parking for road cyclists is generally available in the towns and villages surrounding Erro, such as Aurizberri, Roncesvalles, or Zubiri, which serve as common starting points for routes. Look for public parking areas within these communities.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Erro?
A: The road cycling experience in Erro is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.96 stars from over 14 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the challenging Pyrenean mountain passes, the stunning ancient woodlands, and the diverse terrain of the Erro and Esteribar valleys.

Q: How challenging are the climbs in Erro for road cyclists?
A: The climbs in Erro are a defining feature of the region's road cycling. The ascent to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4817151" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Erro&lt;/a&gt;, for example, presents a moderate but rewarding challenge with an average gradient of around 4.9% over 2.9 kilometers. Many routes include significant elevation gains, making them suitable for cyclists seeking a demanding ride.

Q: Are there any less strenuous road cycling options for beginners in Erro?
A: While Erro is known for its challenging Pyrenean terrain, offering 13 difficult and 8 moderate routes, options specifically designed for beginners are limited. The region's focus is on routes with notable elevation gain. Cyclists new to the area might consider shorter sections of the moderate routes or routes in flatter nearby valleys if available.
tips: The Col de Sorogain is located in the Spanish Basque Country not far from the border with France. From the village of Urepel the climb is 7.2 kilometers long with an average of 7.6% including some passages at 18%.
Small authentic village in the interior of the Basque Country. Nestled on an open plateau in the valley of the same name, it lies at the foot of the border ridge.
